expectations.text: Names the visual hierarchy problem explicitly
expectations.evidence: 'The problem is flat visual hierarchy — every element on your page is competing for attention at the same visual weight'

expectations.text: Mentions at least two of the three hierarchy tools: font size, font weight, color/contrast
expectations.evidence: 'Use all three hierarchy tools together — font size, font weight, and color'

expectations.text: Discusses different levels of content importance (primary, secondary, tertiary)
expectations.evidence: Explicitly grades stat values as primary (large, bold, dark) and labels as secondary (small, 12px, muted grey); also distinguishes primary/secondary table columns

expectations.text: Gives specific actionable recommendations with values (e.g., font weights 600-700, grey for secondary text)
expectations.evidence: Stat value: '32–40px, heavy weight (700)'; stat label: '12px, normal weight, muted grey (#6B7280)'; title: '28–32px, bold (700)'

expectations.text: Recommends HSL format with explanation of why
expectations.evidence: 'Switch to HSL...Hex and RGB are machine values with no human-readable structure...HSL maps to how you perceive color' — leads with HSL, explains why it enables systematic scale-building

expectations.text: Explains a multi-shade scale (e.g., 50 to 900, or 9 shades) with different use cases for light vs dark shades
expectations.evidence: 'Build Your 9-Shade Primary Blue Scale' with CSS custom properties from --blue-50 to --blue-900, each annotated with use case (e.g., 'page-level tinted backgrounds', 'main brand color', 'button hover state')

expectations.text: Mentions saturation needs to be adjusted as lightness changes to prevent washed-out shades
expectations.evidence: 'The Perceptual Lightness Problem — Don't Ignore It...As you go lighter: increase saturation to compensate. A blue-50 at low saturation looks like a grey fog — push saturation up toward 96–100%'

expectations.text: Mentions the palette needs more than primary blue: including greys and/or semantic colors (danger, success, warning)
expectations.evidence: 'Three Color Categories You Need: 1. Greys (8–10 shades)...2. Primary Color (9 shades)...3. Semantic / Accent Colors: Danger/Error, Warning, Success'

expectations.text: Identifies that full-width text is too wide and recommends a maximum line length (around 45-75 characters or ~65ch)
expectations.evidence: 'Line Length Control (Max-Width)...A full-width container...will produce lines well beyond 75 characters...max-width: 65ch is an excellent default...The canonical target is 45–75 characters per line'

expectations.text: Identifies that 1.2 line-height is too tight for body text and recommends a higher value (around 1.5-1.6)
expectations.evidence: 'Your 1.2 line-height is the correct value for large display and headline text, not body text...For a normal column width, use line-height: 1.5 or line-height: 1.6'

expectations.text: Explains that line-height should be proportional to line length (wider lines need more line-height)
expectations.evidence: 'Because your container is currently full-width (long lines), you should actually go even higher: 1.7 until you fix the container width. Once you constrain the line length with a max-width, you can dial back to 1.5–1.6'

expectations.text: Gives specific numeric recommendations (e.g., max-width: 65ch, line-height: 1.5)
expectations.evidence: CSS block: `p { max-width: 65ch; line-height: 1.6; }` provided explicitly

expectations.text: Identifies the issue as ambiguous spacing or unclear proximity between related elements
expectations.evidence: Section header: 'Ambiguous Spacing — Significant. When the space between a label and its input is the same as the space between two unrelated form groups, the eye has no visual cue to determine which label belongs to which field'

expectations.text: Explains that related elements (label + input) should be closer to each other than to unrelated elements
expectations.evidence: 'This is the Gestalt principle of proximity: elements should be closer to what they belong to than to what they don't'

expectations.text: Gives specific spacing values for label-to-input (small) vs group-to-group (larger)
expectations.evidence: Table: 'Label → its input: 4px–8px; Between form groups: 16px–24px; Between page sections: 48px–96px' with CSS example: `.form-label { margin-bottom: 4px; }`, `.form-group { margin-bottom: 24px; }`

expectations.text: Explains that shadow size/softness communicates elevation — closer elements have larger, softer shadows
expectations.evidence: 'From Refactoring UI: closer = more prominent = larger, softer shadow...Shadows don't just add decoration — they convey how high an element floats above the page surface'

expectations.text: Justifies why the modal should have a larger/heavier shadow than the tooltip based on elevation
expectations.evidence: 'Modals sit at the top of the z-layer stack...represent the highest-elevation element in most UIs'; 'Tooltips are closer in character to dropdowns and popovers — small, contextual, lightweight'

expectations.text: Includes specific CSS box-shadow values for at least one element
expectations.evidence: Modal: `0 20px 25px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1), 0 10px 10px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.04)`; Tooltip: `0 4px 6px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.07), 0 2px 4px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.06)` — both provided

expectations.text: Recommends building a reusable shadow scale/system rather than ad-hoc values
expectations.evidence: 'Build a Named Elevation System...define a small set of named levels: --shadow-sm, --shadow-md, --shadow-lg, --shadow-xl, --shadow-2xl' with complete CSS custom properties

expectations.text: Frames the empty state as an important design opportunity, not just a fallback
expectations.evidence: 'Don't Overlook Empty States — a blank white area is a missed design opportunity, often the first thing new users see'; also: 'Treat Every Empty State as a Feature: Empty states aren't an afterthought — they're a product design moment'

expectations.text: Mentions at least two of the three components: acknowledging emptiness, explaining what will appear, providing a call-to-action
expectations.evidence: All three listed explicitly: '1. Acknowledge that nothing is here yet; 2. Explain what will appear here; 3. Offer a clear call-to-action to create the first item'

expectations.text: Provides copy guidance with examples or principles (avoid terse messages like 'No items found')
expectations.evidence: Direct contrast example: '❌ No items found. ✅ You haven't added anything yet. Create your first one to get started.' — exactly the principle + negative/positive example format called for

expectations.text: Identifies the issue as decision fatigue caused by unlimited choices, not a lack of design skill
expectations.evidence: 'The paralysis you're feeling is a systems problem, not a taste problem. When you pick a font size or color from an unlimited pool of possible values, of course you'll second-guess yourself — the design space is infinite'

expectations.text: Recommends defining design systems upfront (type scale, color palette, spacing scale)
expectations.evidence: Table: 'Systems to define before you start designing: Font sizes (12,14,16,18,20,24,...), Font weights (400,500,600,700), Spacing/padding (4,8,12,16,...), Border radii, Shadows'

expectations.text: Explains how constrained options make decisions easier — process of elimination from a small set
expectations.evidence: 'You're not searching infinite space anymore. You're doing binary elimination between 2–3 options. This is fast and produces confident decisions' — exact mechanism described

expectations.text: Names the visual hierarchy problem explicitly
expectations.evidence: 'What you're describing is a lack of visual hierarchy — the page has no clear signal telling the user what's important'

expectations.text: Mentions at least two of the three hierarchy tools: font size, font weight, color/contrast
expectations.evidence: Covers 'Uniform Font Size and Weight' and 'Same Color/Tone for Everything' as two root causes, addressing all three tools

expectations.text: Discusses different levels of content importance (primary, secondary, tertiary)
expectations.evidence: 'Think in Three Levels: Primary (must-see), Secondary (context), Tertiary (background detail)' — explicitly named

expectations.text: Gives specific actionable recommendations with values (e.g., font weights 600-700, grey for secondary text)
expectations.evidence: Gives size ranges ('32–48px', '24–48px') but uses 'bold' rather than specific weight numbers (600/700); colors described as 'muted, lighter gray' and 'very light gray' without hex values. Falls short of the specificity level called for.

expectations.text: Recommends HSL format with explanation of why
expectations.evidence: HSL appears only under 'Method B: Manual HSL Approach' as a secondary option after hex-based tool recommendations. No explanation of why HSL is superior — just a mechanical description of the hue/saturation/lightness axes. Primary recommendation is hex-based tools.

expectations.text: Explains a multi-shade scale (e.g., 50 to 900, or 9 shades) with different use cases for light vs dark shades
expectations.evidence: 'Build a 9–10 Step Scale' with a full table from Blue 50 to Blue 900, each row annotated with a use case (e.g., 'Page backgrounds, tinted sections', 'Primary CTA buttons')

expectations.text: Mentions saturation needs to be adjusted as lightness changes to prevent washed-out shades
expectations.evidence: Mentions hue rotation ('Slightly shift hue warmer at dark end, cooler at light end') but never addresses saturation adjustment. The perceptual lightness issue is not explained.

expectations.text: Mentions the palette needs more than primary blue: including greys and/or semantic colors (danger, success, warning)
expectations.evidence: 'Complementary Colors: Neutrals (gray scale with slight blue tint); Semantic Colors: Success/Green, Warning/Amber, Error/Red'

expectations.text: Identifies that full-width text is too wide and recommends a maximum line length (around 45-75 characters or ~65ch)
expectations.evidence: 'max-width: 65ch (or roughly 680px)...The readable ideal is 45–75 characters per line (roughly 60ch)'

expectations.text: Identifies that 1.2 line-height is too tight for body text and recommends a higher value (around 1.5-1.6)
expectations.evidence: '1.2 line-height is appropriate for headings, not body text...Use 1.5 to 1.7 line-height for body text. A value of 1.6 is a solid default'

expectations.text: Explains that line-height should be proportional to line length (wider lines need more line-height)
expectations.evidence: Response explains the two issues independently but never connects them — never states that wider lines need more line-height. Says 'Line height affects vertical rhythm' and 'Line length affects how easily the eye returns to the next line' but these are treated as separate issues, not proportional factors.

expectations.text: Gives specific numeric recommendations (e.g., max-width: 65ch, line-height: 1.5)
expectations.evidence: CSS blocks: `line-height: 1.6` and `max-width: 65ch` both provided explicitly

expectations.text: Identifies the issue as ambiguous spacing or unclear proximity between related elements
expectations.evidence: 'The Core Problem: Proximity. The visual distance between a label and its input should be smaller than the distance between consecutive form groups. If all gaps are equal, the eye can't group them.'

expectations.text: Explains that related elements (label + input) should be closer to each other than to unrelated elements
expectations.evidence: 'Use the law of proximity deliberately...Label→Input: close (4–6px gap); larger gap between groups (24–32px)'

expectations.text: Gives specific spacing values for label-to-input (small) vs group-to-group (larger)
expectations.evidence: 'Label → Input gap: 4px–8px; Input → Next label gap: 20px–32px' with CSS: `label { margin-bottom: 4px }`, `.form-group { margin-bottom: 24px }`

expectations.text: Explains that shadow size/softness communicates elevation — closer elements have larger, softer shadows
expectations.evidence: 'Objects close to a surface cast short, tight, dark shadows. Objects far from a surface cast long, wide, diffuse shadows.' — clear physics analogy explaining the relationship

expectations.text: Justifies why the modal should have a larger/heavier shadow than the tooltip based on elevation
expectations.evidence: 'Elevation Hierarchy: Modals represent high elevation...Tooltips are low elevation — a small label that barely lifts off the surface. Matching shadow values collapses this distinction'

expectations.text: Includes specific CSS box-shadow values for at least one element
expectations.evidence: Modal: `0 20px 60px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.30), 0 8px 20px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15)`; Tooltip: `0 4px 12px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15)` — both provided

expectations.text: Recommends building a reusable shadow scale/system rather than ad-hoc values
expectations.evidence: 'Use the same shadow system — not the same shadow value. Define a shadow scale (like shadow-sm, shadow-md, shadow-xl) and assign modals to the highest tier and tooltips to a lower tier'

expectations.text: Frames the empty state as an important design opportunity, not just a fallback
expectations.evidence: 'A blank white area is a missed opportunity. Empty states are actually prime real estate for guiding users and setting expectations'

expectations.text: Mentions at least two of the three components: acknowledging emptiness, explaining what will appear, providing a call-to-action
expectations.evidence: 'Explain What Goes Here' and 'Provide a Call to Action' are the first two principles; implicitly acknowledges emptiness via 'No projects yet' example headline

expectations.text: Provides copy guidance with examples or principles (avoid terse messages like 'No items found')
expectations.evidence: 'A brief headline like No projects yet paired with a short description (Create a project to start organizing your work) makes the purpose immediately clear' — provides concrete positive example copy

expectations.text: Identifies the issue as decision fatigue caused by unlimited choices, not a lack of design skill
expectations.evidence: 'The paralysis comes from treating each choice as a blank canvas. Constraints are liberating, not limiting' — correctly reframes paralysis as a systems problem

expectations.text: Recommends defining design systems upfront (type scale, color palette, spacing scale)
expectations.evidence: 'Define a system upfront: a fixed type scale (5–7 sizes), a limited color palette (~15 colors), and an 8px spacing grid'

expectations.text: Explains how constrained options make decisions easier — process of elimination from a small set
expectations.evidence: 'Stop making decisions in isolation — build constraints first'; 'Pick a reasonable default and ship'; the response explains constraints reduce the decision space but doesn't explicitly describe binary elimination
